children.name: Merops
children.description: Typical bee-eaters — brilliantly coloured, slender birds specialised for catching bees, wasps, and other flying insects on the wing. They remove the sting by rubbing the prey against a perch before swallowing. Their dazzling plumage — combinations of green, blue, yellow, and chestnut — make them among the most beautiful of all birds. Most species are migratory, wintering in Africa.

children.name: Merops apiaster
children.description: A spectacular summer visitor to southern and central Europe. Golden-yellow throat, turquoise belly, chestnut crown, and a black eye-stripe. Nests in burrows in sand banks. Its far-carrying 'prruuk-prruuk' call is a sound of Mediterranean summer. Increasingly recorded in Sweden as climate warms.

children.name: Merops superciliosus
children.description: Found across sub-Saharan Africa and Madagascar east to Indonesia. Green above, pale below with a yellow throat and a distinctive white supercilium. More olive-toned than the European Bee-eater. Inhabits savanna, woodland, and forest edges.

children.name: Merops viridis
children.description: A striking bee-eater of Southeast Asia. Blue throat, chestnut crown, and green upperparts. Nests in burrows in sandy banks. Often seen perched on wires and dead branches in open country.

children.name: Merops nubicus
children.description: A spectacular red-and-pink bee-eater of sub-Saharan Africa. Brilliant carmine-red plumage with a greenish-blue crown and rump. Breeds in huge colonies in river banks, sometimes numbering thousands of birds. Feeds on swarms of locusts and bees.

children.name: Merops orientalis
children.description: A small, grass-green bee-eater found from North Africa through the Middle East to India and Southeast Asia. Green body, turquoise throat, and a distinctive black neck band. Often seen in small groups perched on wires, giving a sharp 'tree-tree' call.

children.name: Merops albicollis
children.description: A migratory bee-eater breeding in the Sahel zone south of the Sahara and wintering in equatorial Africa. Striking pattern: green crown, black mask, white throat, and pale blue underparts. Nests in large colonies in sandy river banks.
